summary:
Hannibal's Crossing of the Alps (218 BC) was the opening strategic gambit of the [[event:1054|Second Punic War]] — a bold march of approximately 40,000 infantry, 8,000 cavalry, and 37 war elephants from Spain through southern Gaul and across the Alps into northern Italy that remains one of the most celebrated military feats in history. By taking the war directly to Italy rather than waiting for Rome to attack Carthage, Hannibal sought to break Rome's Italian alliances and force a negotiated peace.

The Alpine crossing took approximately fifteen days and cost enormous losses — to ambushes by mountain tribes, rockslides, and the brutal conditions. Ancient sources give figures of roughly half the army lost in the crossing, though modern historians debate these numbers. The elephants proved unexpectedly well-suited to the cold; most survived the initial crossing though most died in the Italian winter that followed. Which specific pass Hannibal used remains one of archaeology's unsolved debates, with competing scholars arguing for passes from the Col du Mont Cenis to the Col de la Traversette.

The psychological impact of Hannibal appearing in northern Italy with an army was enormous — Romans had not expected this line of attack. His subsequent victories over Roman armies at the Trebia, [[event:1061|Battle of Lake Trasimene]], and [[event:1056|Battle of Cannae]] demonstrated that the gamble had paid off strategically. The Alps crossing became a defining image of audacious generalship, later inspiring Napoleon's crossing of the Great St. Bernard Pass in 1800.
